topics.topic_name: India as a Mega Bio-diversity Country

content.text: India is one of the 12 mega bio-diversity countries of the world. With about 47,000 plant species, India occupies the tenth place in the world and fourth in Asia in plant diversity. The country has approximately 15,000 flowering plants, accounting for 6 percent of the world's total number of flowering plants. In addition, India has many non-flowering plants such as ferns, algae, and fungi.

India also has approximately 90,000 species of animals, along with a rich variety of fish in its fresh and marine waters. This vast biodiversity results from India's diverse geographical features including varying altitudes, climates, and soil types across different regions.

Plant community that has grown naturally without human aid and has been left undisturbed by humans for a long time, termed virgin vegetation. Cultivated crops and fruits do not constitute natural vegetation.

The virgin vegetation purely from India is known as endemic or indigenous species. Those plants that have come from outside India are termed exotic plants. Similarly, the flora refers to plants of a particular region or period, while fauna refers to animal species.

notes.section_title: Types of Natural Vegetation in India

content.text: The major types of vegetation identified in India are classified based on rainfall, climate, and altitude. These include Tropical Evergreen Forests, Tropical Deciduous Forests, Tropical Thorn Forests and Scrubs, Montane Forests, and Mangrove Forests. Each type supports distinct animal and plant communities suited to their environmental conditions.

Tropical Evergreen Forests are restricted to heavy rainfall areas of the Western Ghats, Lakshadweep and Andaman-Nicobar islands, upper parts of Assam, and Tamil Nadu coast. They are at their best in areas receiving more than 200 cm of rainfall with a short dry season. Trees reach great heights up to 60 metres or more.

Since the region is warm and wet throughout the year, these forests have luxuriant vegetation of all kinds — trees, shrubs, and creepers giving them a multilayered structure. Trees have no definite time to shed their leaves, making these forests appear green all year round.

Commercially important trees include ebony, mahogany, rosewood, rubber, and cinchona. Animal fauna includes elephants, monkeys, lemurs, deer, and one-horned rhinoceroses in Assam and West Bengal jungles. Abundant birds, bats, sloths, scorpions, and snails are also found.

Tropical Deciduous Forests are the most widespread forests of India, also called monsoon forests. They spread over regions receiving rainfall between 200 cm and 70 cm. Trees shed their leaves for about six to eight weeks in dry summer. These forests are further divided into moist and dry deciduous based on water availability.

Moist Deciduous Forests are found in areas receiving rainfall between 200 and 100 cm, mostly in northeastern states, foothills of Himalayas, Jharkhand, West Odisha, Chhattisgarh, and eastern slopes of Western Ghats. Teak is the most dominant species, with bamboos, sal, shisham, sandalwood, khair, kusum, arjun, and mulberry as commercially important trees.

Dry Deciduous Forests are found in areas with rainfall between 100 cm and 70 cm, in rainier parts of Peninsular plateau and plains of Bihar and Uttar Pradesh. Large parts have been cleared for cultivation and some for grazing. Common animals include lions, tigers, pigs, deer, and elephants. Diverse birds, lizards, snakes, and tortoises are also present.

In regions with less than 70 cm of rainfall, natural vegetation consists of thorny trees and bushes. This type is found in the northwestern part of India, including semi-arid areas of Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Uttar Pradesh, and Haryana. Acacias, palms, euphorbias, and cacti are the main plant species.

Trees are scattered with long roots penetrating deep into soil to get moisture. Stems are succulent to conserve water. Leaves are mostly thick and small to minimise evaporation. Common animals are rats, mice, rabbits, foxes, wolves, tigers, lions, wild ass, horses, and camels.

In mountainous areas, decreasing temperature with increasing altitude leads to corresponding changes in natural vegetation, creating a succession of vegetation belts. Wet temperate forests with evergreen broad-leaf trees like oaks and chestnuts are found between 1000 and 2000 metres.

Between 1500 and 3000 metres, temperate forests containing coniferous trees like pine, deodar, silver fir, spruce, and cedar are found, covering mostly southern slopes of Himalayas and high-altitude areas. At higher elevations above 3,600 metres, temperate forests give way to Alpine vegetation including silver fir, junipers, pines, and birches. These become progressively stunted approaching the snowline, eventually merging into Alpine grasslands used for grazing by nomadic tribes like Gujjars and Bakarwals.
Common animals in these forests include Kashmir stag, spotted deer, wild sheep, Tibetan antelope, yak, snow leopard, squirrels, shaggy-horned wild ibex, bears, and rare red pandas.

Mangrove tidal forests are found in coastal areas influenced by tides where mud and silt accumulate. Dense mangroves are common with roots submerged under water. The deltas of the Ganga, Mahanadi, Krishna, Godavari, and Kaveri are covered by such vegetation. In the Ganga-Brahmaputra delta, sundari trees are found providing durable hard timber. Palm, coconut, keora, and agar also grow in some delta parts.

Royal Bengal Tigers are the famous animals in these forests. Turtles, crocodiles, gharials, and snakes are also found. These ecosystems are crucial for fish breeding and are ecologically sensitive areas.

India is known for its herbs and spices from ancient times, with at least 500 in regular use. According to the World Conservation Union, 352 medicinal plants are in India, of which 52 are critically threatened and 49 endangered. Approximately 2,000 plants have been described in Ayurveda, India's traditional medicine system.

Examples of important medicinal plants include Sarpagandha (used to treat blood pressure and found only in India), Jamun (juice prevents diabetes and has digestive properties), Arjun (fresh leaf juice cures heart problems and regulates blood pressure), Babool (leaves cure eye sores and gum used as tonic), Neem (high antibiotic and antibacterial properties), Tulsi (cures cough and cold), and Kachnar (cures asthma and ulcers with digestive benefits).

content.title: Dorothy Hodgkin and Vitamin B12
content.text: Dorothy Hodgkin was a Nobel Prize-winning chemist (1964) who studied the structure of Vitamin B12, essential for proper body functioning. Her research contributes to understanding medicinal compounds important for health.

India possesses approximately 90,000 animal species. The country has about 2,000 species of birds constituting 13% of the world's total. There are 2,546 species of fish accounting for nearly 12% of the world's stock. India also shares between 5 and 8 percent of the world's amphibians, reptiles, and mammals.

Elephants are the most majestic animals among the mammals, found in hot wet forests of Assam, Karnataka, and Kerala. One-horned rhinoceroses live in swampy and marshy lands of Assam and West Bengal. Wild ass and camels inhabit arid areas of Rann of Kachchh and Thar Desert respectively.

Indian bison, nilgai (blue bull), chousingha (four-horned antelope), gazelle, and different species of deer are found across India. Several species of monkeys are also present. The country also has several species of monkeys distributed across different regions.

India is the only country in the world having both tigers and lions. The natural habitat of the Indian lion is the Gir Forest in Gujarat, the last remaining habitat of the Asiatic lion. Tigers are found in forests of Madhya Pradesh, Sundarbans of West Bengal, and Himalayan regions.

Leopards are important members of the cat family and are members of prey animals. They are distributed across various regions where suitable forest habitat exists. These apex predators play crucial roles in maintaining ecological balance.

The Himalayas harbour a hardy range of animals surviving extreme cold. Ladakh's freezing high altitudes are home to yak (shaggy horned wild ox weighing around one tonne), Tibetan antelope, bharal (blue sheep), wild sheep, and kiang (Tibetan wild ass). The ibex, bear, snow-leopard, and rare red panda are found in certain pockets.

In rivers, lakes, and coastal areas, turtles, crocodiles, and gharials are found. The gharial is the only representative of a variety of crocodile found in the world today. Bird life in India is colourful with peacocks, pheasants, ducks, parakeets, cranes, and pigeons inhabiting forests and wetlands.

During winter, migratory birds like Siberian Crane come in large numbers to wetlands such as the Rann of Kachchh. Flamingos with brilliant pink plumage come in thousands to build nest mounds from salty mud in regions where desert meets sea.

Every species has a role to play in the ecosystem. We have selected our crops from a bio-diverse environment from the reserve of edible plants. We have experimented and selected many medicinal plants. Animals were selected from nature's stock as milch animals, providing draught power, transportation, meat, and eggs. Fish provide nutritive food. Many insects help in pollination of crops and fruit trees.

Conservation is essential because excessive exploitation of plant and animal resources by humans has disturbed the ecosystem. Approximately 1,300 plant species are endangered and 20 species are extinct. Many animal species are also endangered or extinct.

The main causes threatening biodiversity are hunting by greedy hunters for commercial purposes, pollution from chemical and industrial waste, acid deposits, introduction of alien species, and reckless cutting of forests to bring land under cultivation and habitation. These activities create imbalance in the ecosystem.

To protect the flora and fauna of the country, the government has established eighteen biosphere reserves. Twelve of these — Sundarbans, Nanda Devi, Gulf of Mannar, Nilgiri, Nokrek, Great Nicobar, Simlipal, Pachmarhi, Achanakmar-Amarkantak, Agasthyamalai, Kangchendzonga, and Panna — have been included in the world network of biosphere reserves. This international recognition reflects India's commitment to conservation.
Protected areas established to conserve biodiversity by protecting flora and fauna in their natural habitats, combining conservation with sustainable development.

Beyond biosphere reserves, India has established 106 National Parks, 573 Wildlife Sanctuaries, and Zoological Gardens to take care of natural heritage. These protected areas serve as refuges for endangered species and help maintain ecological integrity.

Financial and technical assistance has been provided to many botanical gardens by the government since 1992. These gardens serve as repositories of plant diversity and research centres for plant conservation and study.

Project Tiger, Project Rhino, Project Great Indian Bustard, and many other eco-developmental projects have been introduced to protect specific endangered species and habitats. These targeted programmes focus on species-specific conservation strategies and habitat protection.

content.title: Project Tiger
content.text: Initiated to protect the endangered tiger population, Project Tiger has established tiger reserves across India to maintain viable tiger populations and their habitats.

The Wildlife Protection Act was implemented in 1972 in India, providing legal framework for conservation. This legislation protects wildlife, regulates hunting, and establishes penalties for violations, forming the backbone of India's wildlife conservation policy.

All of us must realise the importance of the natural ecosystem for our own survival. It is possible to prevent indiscriminate destruction of the natural environment through immediate action. Conservation of biodiversity is not just about protecting plants and animals; it is about ensuring the sustainability of human civilization and maintaining the ecological balance that supports all life on Earth.

glossary.definition: Plant community that has grown naturally without human aid and remains undisturbed for a long time; termed virgin vegetation. Does not include cultivated crops or fruits.
Refers to plants of a particular region or period; used to describe the plant species composition of an area.
Refers to animal species of a particular region or period; used to describe the animal composition of an area.
Plant or animal species that are purely found in India and nowhere else in the world; also called indigenous species.
Plant or animal species that have originated from outside India and been introduced into the country.
Forests in high-rainfall areas (>200 cm) that remain green year-round with no definite leaf-shedding season; found in Western Ghats, Assam, and island groups.
Forests in moderate rainfall areas (70-200 cm) where trees shed leaves for 6-8 weeks in dry season; most widespread in India.
Deciduous forests in areas receiving 100-200 cm rainfall; found in northeastern states and foothills; dominated by teak trees.
Deciduous forests in areas receiving 70-100 cm rainfall; found in Peninsular plateau and northern plains; trees shed leaves more extensively.
Scrubland and thorny vegetation in arid regions (<70 cm rainfall) with scattered trees, succulent stems, and small leaves adapted to water scarcity.
Forests in mountainous regions where vegetation changes with altitude, progressing from temperate forests to Alpine vegetation with increasing elevation.
Forests found at high altitudes (1500-3000m) containing coniferous trees like pine, deodar, spruce, and cedar adapted to cool climates.
Vegetation found at very high altitudes (>3,600m) including stunted trees, shrubs, and grasslands adapted to extreme cold and thin air.
Tidal forests found in coastal areas with muddy and silty soils; trees have roots submerged in saltwater; important for fish breeding.
The variety of plant and animal species within a region or ecosystem; encompasses genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity.
Protected areas established to conserve biodiversity while combining conservation with sustainable development of surrounding areas.
Protected area established by government with strict protection for flora and fauna; public access limited for conservation purposes.
Protected area where wildlife is conserved with some restrictions on human activity; typically smaller and less strictly protected than national parks.
Species with population numbers severely reduced due to habitat loss, hunting, or other threats, facing risk of extinction.
Species that no longer exists; all members of the species have died, eliminating it from the Earth.
Natural vegetation that has grown undisturbed by humans for a long period, representing original plant communities of an area.

summary_points: India is one of 12 mega bio-diversity countries with 47,000 plant species (10th globally, 4th in Asia), 15,000 flowering plants (6% of world total), and 90,000 animal species including 2,000 bird species (13% of world) and 2,546 fish species (12% of world stock).

Five major vegetation types are identified based on rainfall and altitude: Tropical Evergreen (>200cm rainfall, Western Ghats), Tropical Deciduous/Monsoon (70-200cm, most widespread), Tropical Thorn/Scrub (<70cm, Rajasthan-Gujarat), Montane (altitude-based succession), and Mangrove (coastal tidal areas).

Tropical Evergreen Forests have multilayered structure with trees reaching 60m+, species like ebony-mahogany-rubber, and fauna including elephants, leopards, and one-horned rhinos; they appear green year-round with no definite leaf-shedding season.

Tropical Deciduous Forests (most widespread) are divided into moist (100-200cm rainfall, teak-dominated) and dry (70-100cm, sal-peepal dominated) with diverse fauna including tigers, lions, elephants, and extensive bird-reptile populations.

Montane Forests show altitude-driven succession: wet temperate (1000-2000m, broad-leaf trees), temperate (1500-3000m, coniferous trees like pine-deodar), and Alpine (>3600m, stunted vegetation-grasslands); fauna includes Kashmir stag, snow leopard, yak, Tibetan antelope.

Mangrove Forests occur in tidal coastal areas (Ganga-Brahmaputra, Krishna-Godavari deltas) with roots submerged in saltwater; support Royal Bengal tigers, crocodiles, gharials, and fish breeding grounds essential for marine ecosystem.

Medicinal plants are central to Indian heritage with 2,000 plants described in Ayurveda; 352 medicinal plants threatened with 52 critically endangered; examples include Sarpagandha (blood pressure), Neem (antibiotic), Tulsi (cold-cough), Arjun (heart health).

India is only country with both tigers and lions; Indian lion habitat is Gir Forest (Gujarat); tigers found in Madhya Pradesh-Sundarbans-Himalayas; other major fauna include elephants (Assam-Karnataka-Kerala), one-horned rhinos (Assam), wild ass-camels (deserts).

Biodiversity conservation is critical as 1,300 plant species are endangered and 20 extinct; threats include hunting, pollution, deforestation, and alien species introduction disrupting ecosystems where every species plays an ecological role.

Government conservation framework includes 18 biosphere reserves (12 internationally recognized including Sundarbans, Nanda Devi, Gulf of Mannar), 106 national parks, 573 wildlife sanctuaries, and targeted projects (Project Tiger, Project Rhino); Wildlife Protection Act (1972) provides legal framework for conservation.
